chunks.text: Six and four. Okay. No
problems. Right. Good. Right, Kelly, we've got the Salvation Army.
Why am I...
Hold on.

Kelly, are you there? Oh, shall I just- We'll start with the Salvation Army, if we may. Charlotte, Kelly's not here. Oh, is it Charlotte? Thanks, Carl. Anna. Oh, Anna? Well done. Right, Anna, we'll have-- We need someone from Salvation Army.

Yes. Yeah. Can I start now? Good morning. Yes. Is that you, Anna? Very good. Fantastic. We've got 10 minutes with you. Cool. Thank you. Mōrena koutou, everybody. Thank you again to the Justice Select Committee for the opportunity to speak to our submission for this bill.

The Salvation Army's mission is to care for people, transform lives and reform society by God's power. We have worked in New Zealand supporting communities in regards to alcohol harm for over a century. And so we are very interested in how our country regulates alcohol and so this is why we've engaged in this submission.

So the Salvation Army, we see alcohol harm across a wide spectrum from our Bridge AOD services, which runs from Kaikohe all the way down to the South Island, to Dunedin, is the last, but covers a wider area than Dunedin. And so we're supporting tangata whai ora in regards to addictions, but also their families.

Not just with alcohol, but with the majority of our tangata whai ora are poly substance users. And then again, we see alcohol harm in regards to property damage, family violence, financial hardship, food insecurity. And so the list goes on in regards to the repercussions of alcohol harm that we're seeing in our communities.

And so we engage heavily in the district licensing process. We've submitted on, I guess, all of the areas in this bill, but I just want to quickly cover a few areas if I have time to, and I'll try and leave time at the end for questions. So we oppose the restrictions on those that can object.

This year, we've appeared before the Auckland DLC, the Tauranga DLC, the Far North DLC, Horowhenua DLC. The list goes on in regards to the DLCs that we engage with, because that's where all of our services are. And the common theme that we see in the objection process is that community organizations like ours, like others that engage in this space, we bear the brunt of alcohol harm. And the realities that we face is very different to the realities that applicants or license holders face.

The DLC is a commission of inquiry, and so their job is to be able to see different angles, and line them up according to the act before a license is given. And by limiting those who can object, you're ultimately limiting the information that goes before a DLC to whether a license is approved or not, or renewed.

And so that's a common theme that we see and a reason why we think we shouldn't limit who can object. Before the Community Participation Bill came into act, the phrase that we used in the act was greater interest. So the unspoken rule was two kilometers. And so what the bill in 2023 did is it removed it.

We know submitters have come before you and talked about 500 meters and one kilometer, but the boundaries of alcohol harm goes far beyond where that particular premise is beyond 500 meters, beyond one kilometer. We would suggest that if you are going to rein it in, rein it in to territorial authorities, and not one kilometer or 500 meters. The other area of the bill that we just wanted to highly speak on was the applicant's right-Of reply.

We're not quite sure of the purpose of that, because in our engagement and DRC process, we've engaged many times with license holders or those that are applying for licenses in regards to our objection before it gets to the DRC. And we're able to talk to applicants around clarifications around what they've applied for, or applicant holders are able to address our concerns and bring in discretionary conditions, and that usually happens between the license inspector or a lawyer.

And we just think that the right of reply will move the process into a legal process and bring back cross-examinations, which we had opposed and are grateful that they've gone. Looking at the time, probably the last thing or two more things that we just want to address is that the LAP process is quite a vigorous process for local councils.

I mean, so there's a research process, and everyone's allowed to engage in the LAP process. And so we think if you limit the effectiveness of the LAP on renewals, that would diminish local democracy, and the ability for those tools to be able to mitigate harm in their communities.

And also, those license holders can also engage in the LAP process as well. The last thing I just want to mention, and I know some of this before, you have talked about the trading hours, is that we would recommend a 9:00 a.m. to 9:00 p.m. national trading hours for off licenses. We know that it's currently in place in Auckland. We did an OIA to the police, and we know that when it came to Auckland, across the police districts, the highest decreases when it came to alcohol-related police incidences decreased significantly for

the three police districts, so Waitematā, Auckland, and County Manukau. The highest was in Northland, but Northland only represents 5% of alcohol-related police incident call-outs, and Auckland, as a whole, represents 30%. And so the highest decreases have been in Auckland, and that's from the hours of 9:00 to 2:00 because that's when majority, I think over 40%, of alcohol-related harm that police attend is between those hours, 9:00 to 2:00 a.m. And so if we rein in those trading hours nationally for off licenses, we think it'd be a great balance

in regards to what the current bill is proposing to bring in those national trading hours. And so, again, our submission sort of outlined the areas that we oppose and those that we agree with. There are areas of the bill that we support. But again, areas that we don't support and think that there are other ways that it can be done that would achieve the object of the Act. So, if there's any questions.

Right. Very good. Thank you very much. Right, I've got a question from Mr. Stevenson. Oh, thank you for your submission. Very helpful. Just want to check, the objection issue, bill actually is drafted as you can object if you live in the territorial authority area or within the kilometer range. So would that satisfy what you're after?

I think if we remove the kilometer range and just leave it to territorial authority. No. Well, this is the point. I just want to make sure you're clear. It actually is an and/or test, so that's to make sure that if you were living on the boundary of an authority but the alcohol outlet is just across the border within two kilometers, then you can still object. Am I making sense?

So I think the bill actually does what I think you want. That's why I just wanted to clarify, because it's-- Yeah, your right to object is if you live in the territorial authority or you're two kilometers or whatever it is from the outlet. So I think it-- That's why I just wanted to check if that would satisfy what you're after. Yeah. As long as it's not-- Because prior to the Community Participation Bill coming in, it was two kilometers from the premise.

Yeah. And so as long as it's not limiting- No... reverting back to that old- Yeah. Okay, cool... yeah. This is a slight change. That's why I just wanted to clarify. But thank you for your submission, and we've read it. Hey, thank you very much, Anna, and joining us this morning. Thank you. Right. Now, I think we're going to Lizzie Strickett. Is that correct?

Can I just ask which officials are in the room? So it's not Kelly, I take it? No, it's Anna. No, it's Anna. It is Anna. We had two Annas back then. Okay, cool. Thanks, Anna. Morena, tātou. Can you hear me? We can. Yeah. And nice to be with you this morning. We've got 10 minutes with you.

Ka pai. All right, let's do this. Tēnā koutou katoa. Ko Lizzie Strickett toku ingoa. I'm appearing today on behalf of Kētua o Matariki, which is the iwi health and social service of Ngāti Paoa in Tāmaki Makaurau. Joining me today is a member of our community, Hiwaati Rangi.

Last year, her kaiako, herself, her hua from her kōhanga, Ngā Whare Maha in Rosedale, joined us in opposing a proposed alcohol outlet opening within walking distance of the kōhanga. And she wanted to be here today to represent her kōhanga and her hapori. So thank you for the opportunity to speak on this bill.

I'll take my submission as read, our submission as read, but just to summarize, Kētua o Matariki supports matua tailhill young parents through hapūtanga and the first 2,000 days of life. And like the submission before us, every day, we see the impacts of alcohol harm on our young whānau, our pipi, and our tamariki.

I'll take my submission as read but want to raise three concerns or three points that I've spoken toFirst, we've got significant concerns that this proposed bill weakens community voice. We understand this government has said that these changes will make our system more efficient, but we believe that efficiency shouldn't come at the cost of democratic participation.

Our concern is that communities already face really significant barriers in participating in current alcohol licensing processes. We're worried about the cumulative effect of introducing additional tests and barriers, particularly as a mana whenua organization, working with whanau in our community, like our kohanga, who already find these processes really difficult to navigate.

So to give you an example of the submission that Hiwa-i-Te-Rangi and her kohanga did with us, the process itself highlighted how difficult their participation was. So they wanted to have a say. We worked with them. They really were actively interested and involved.

We went there to present our submission together, and we got there and the kohanga was advised that it would be best for them and their whanau not to attend the hearing room because the council environment wasn't set up for community participation, particularly for young families. So instead, they were asked to wait outside while us, as a few representatives, spoke on their behalf.

When we presented the concerns of the kohanga, and for us as mana whenua with active interest in that area, we were asked questions like, "You represent a mana whenua organization. How am I supposed to even know that you exist?" And that experience, to speak to your point, that the question that you raised on district boundaries, the question that I have back to that is, what does this mean for mana whenua organizations like ourselves, who are actively involved in these communities, who have ancestral ties, who have tamariki, who

have whakapapa to Ngati Paoa, who are within these communities, but we might not have a physical address there. What will this bill mean for them and for us? So that experience left us reflecting on how difficult it already is for our whanau to have a say. People have to take time off work, arrange childcare, navigate these unfamiliar processes.

So, I guess this bill recognizes geography, but our question is around also whakapapa as well. And we drew on evidence within our submission, our written submission, that this isn't a widespread problem in terms of irrelevant community objection. Certainly in Auckland, there's some evidence that shows that when 500 rejections were reviewed, none were found to be irrelevant, and only two came outside of Auckland.

Our second concern is around what this bill will mean for the increased availability and normalization of alcohol. We're already really concerned about introducing alcohol into everyday community spaces like barbershops, and we've got really good relationships with our barbers within our community, and they've said to us that they don't want this.

For many Maori men, barbershops isn't just a business or a place to get a haircut. They tell us that it's a place for whakawhanaungatanga and for connection. And one of our local barbers said this space is more than just guys coming to get their monthly haircuts. It's a place where they can talk and feel safe and be uplifted.

And so we should be protecting these spaces and not normalizing alcohol within them. Thirdly, this bill would disproportionately affect communities already carrying the greatest burden of harm. For a lot of the whanau that we work with, fetal alcohol spectrum disorder is something that we see a lot, and throughout our FASD and whanau support work, we're hearing directly from young parents living with those challenges.

So to give you an example, at a recent FASD wananga that we held at our community space, Te Whaipiringa in GI, in Glen Innes in Tamaki, whanau spoke about alcohol being much easier to access than counseling in that area, and support during those difficult periods. And to give you a quote, one of the mums said to us, "I was going through a really hard time in my life when I was pregnant, but there was three bottle stores within walking distance of where I lived, but counseling was too expensive." So many of those whanau are thankfully

now on their healing journeys and working really hard to create healthier futures for their tamariki and mokopuna. But we're concerned that this bill will make their journeys much harder by increasing the visibility, the accessibility, and normalization of alcohol in everyday life. So if the committee chooses to progress this bill, we really urge that you retain those broad community rights to object, that the previous submitter has spoken to, but that there has to be more consideration for iwi and for hapu like ourselves, who might not have a physical address

within that area, but has active interest. And to the point around if you don't live there but want to object and live within two kilometers, my question is, what does this mean for-- in the example I've given you of her kohanga, we've got many whanau who, many tamariki who live in multiple dwellings.

Some live in other suburbs because their parents might live elsewhere, but they really wanted to have a say in that objection process. So what would this mean for them? We also urge that you protect local alcohol policies as an important expression of community voice and local decision-making, and look into those trading hours preventing further expansion into everyday community spaces. So ultimately, we believe alcohol policy should be guided by the well-being of our communities and not by the convenience of the alcohol industrySo I'm willing to take

questions. We've got about three minutes, don't we? All right. Hey, thanks, Lizzie. Right. Any questions from anyone? Don't think so. Lizzie, why do you think that you- Oh, there's a question. Oh, sorry. I've got a question here. Yeah. Miss Kahu Carter.

Go ahead, Rachel. Kia ora, Lizzie. Thank you so much. I just wanted to say thank you for speaking on behalf of Ngā Whare Maha. I used to be a kaiako there, and my kids went there as well. So, lovely to see whānau. So, in terms of the whānau living outside the territory or the one-kilometer zone, do you think that this should be opened up to the whole of Aotearoa? Or what territories do you think that it should be open to?

Well, I think it should be based on what the community feel, because I just think, like in the instance of our whānau and these tamariki, they live in multiple dwellings, and it should be opened up. Kia ora. All right. Hey, thank you, Lizzie. I really appreciate you coming on this morning.
That's very nice. And it was a great presentation. Thank you. Thank you. Right. Now we're going to Clubs New Zealand. Lucy, are you with us, Lucy?

All right. Good morning. Good morning. We're watching. We are watching, and we've got 10 minutes with you. Looking forward to hearing what you've got to say. Brilliant. Thank you. And good morning, and thank you for the opportunity to appear today. My name is Lucy Wateriss, Chief Executive of Clubs New Zealand.

We represent 300 clubs across the country, ranging from small groups of a few dozen members who might get together a few times a week, through to large community hubs of over 13,000 members. Regardless of size, they are places where New Zealanders gather to socialize, play sport, share meals, access affordable entertainment, and find community.

Clubs New Zealand supports this bill. We think it strikes the right balance between harm minimization and practical improvements for responsible operators. Clubs are generally low risk. They are member-governed, not for profit, and deeply embedded within their communities.

That model naturally supports responsible service, and it is a responsibility that we take seriously. As always, I take our written submission as read, and in the interest of time, want to focus today on why change is needed, particularly for club licenses and special licenses.

The provision allowing clubs to choose between a club license and a non-license introduces something that clubs have not had in a very long time, and that is choice. We strongly support this provision. It provides a lawful and practical pathway for some clubs to better meet community needs.

But it's important that it remains a genuine choice, and not something that clubs feel pushed toward by reporting agencies or because the existing settings are too restrictive. We do believe that clear transitional provisions need to be included within the bill.

If a club chooses to move to a non-license, it should be treated as a transition, not a new license, and not a full reset. We do believe that its compliance history should be retained. While we strongly support clubs having the opportunity to choose between a club license and a non-license, we do believe that the underlying issues are found within Section 60 of the act itself, and they should be addressed as well.

In our view, Section 60 is the root cause of the many challenges that clubs face today. It restricts who a club can serve in ways that no longer reflect how communities operate. That, in turn, limits a club's ability to host events, attract participation, and remain sustainable.

Clubs today are not exclusive and closed environments. They are often the only venues for community events, such as funerals, prize givings, fundraisers, and local gatherings. 300,000 New Zealanders choose to belong to one or more of our clubs, 4,000 people are employed within our clubs, and more than 6,000 volunteers give their time to our clubs every year.

Yet to meet community expectations, clubs are often forced into what I would describe as complex workarounds of the law. It is the restrictive nature of Section 60 that drives heavy reliance on special licenses. And I do want to be clear that clubs are not applying for special licenses because they want to. They are doing so to meet community need and to comply with the law. This often means applying for special licenses for routine, low-risk events where alcohol is incidental.

In some parts of the country, we've seen informal limits being applied to how many special licenses a club can apply for in one year. And you can just imagine the club newsletter going out, asking people to please delay their funerals or special occasions to the following year because the club has hit its special license limit.

That sounds absurd to us, because it is.While it may sound macabre, funerals are actually a good example of where clubs use special licenses. That they're regularly hosting funerals for members of the club or the wider community, and in many cases, the club is such an important part of the member's life, it's where they choose to say farewell.

But funerals are not events that we book weeks in advance, and the 20 working-day requirement for a special license is often impractical. And while waivers exist, they come with additional costs, and that adds stress and cost at an already difficult time for people.

Another great example that I heard came from the House during the first reading of the bill. There was discussion around boating clubs needing special licenses for events like the Wotton Cup, where one club hosts another and another club visits. The issue here is not the participants in that boating event, it's the supporters, it's the family members, the friends, the wider community that turn up to support the event. Those people are often not members themselves of either clubs. So to remain compliant, the club is left with little choice but to apply for a

special license to cover what is, in effect, a normal community activity. That is where the system becomes impractical because we're requiring already licensed low-risk clubs to repeatedly apply for special licenses for events that we believe pose no additional risk.

We believe that there is a more practical approach through expanding the definition of authorized visitor under Section 60 to include any person attending a function or club event similar to jurisdictions like Queensland. Clubs are unique. We are not-for-profit, member-based community venues. But we are not immune to the pressures facing the wider hospitality sector.

In my role, I'm often asked why are clubs struggling, why are RSAs folding. And the reality is, there is no one single answer to that, but perception plays a very big role. Imagine turning up to a club for the first time, being faced with a closed door, a doorbell, a swipe card access, a large members only sign. I myself have been chased across a club by the office lady. She was very well-intentioned, but asking me to sign the sign-in book.

That is not welcoming, and it's a barrier before you've even stepped in the door. But those barriers exist because clubs are trying to comply with Section 60. At the same time, we are trying to evolve. We have clubs trying to introduce new membership categories. They are trying to be more open and accessible because they know they need to attract new members to survive.

But too often those efforts are treated by reporting agencies with suspicion, or they're interpreted as attempts to work around the law. This creates a tension, and we need to rebalance the settings so that clubs can genuinely welcome people in so that becoming a member feels like a natural next step and not a compliance exercise. Because ultimately, if clubs cannot attract new members, they cannot remain sustainable.

And if they're not sustainable, then we lose that really important community infrastructure that they provide. So in summary, we support this bill. We welcome the introduction of choice for clubs. But we strongly encourage the committee to ensure that there are fair transitional provisions for clubs that do choose a non-license.

Amend Section 60 to better reflect how clubs operate today and address the practical realities of special licenses. These changes would reduce unnecessary burden, improve consistency, and ensure clubs can continue serving their communities in a safe and responsible way. Thank you.

Hey, thanks, Lucy. Right. Questions, members? Right. Koharangi Kaa has got one. Thanks so much, Lucy. I'm just interested to know, in those 300 clubs, is it volunteers who are actually serving alcohol to members and their guests? In some of them- Some of them or all?

Yes, it is. It largely depends on the size of the club. Yeah. So in the club with 13,000 members that I mentioned, they have paid staff that are all trained. However, in those smaller clubs where it might only be a couple of hundred members, then you're looking at volunteers.

However, those volunteers are still all trained. There are roles within a club license where you don't have to have a duty manager on the premises at all times. However, we are finding that that is becoming a default condition that's placed on most club licenses.

Thank you. I just have another question, if that's all right, Mr. Chair. Okay. Yeah. So whenever I go to a club, I always notice that the alcohol's a lot cheaper than, say, a bar that's next door or in the area. Have you ever looked into the difference in price between what's the price of alcohol in a club compared to the neighboring or the average of the same beverage?

We haven't done a full research into that. It's a challenging one because I've actually had people believe that there is a legal requirement for clubs to be cheaper than other premises, which is simply not the case. And so we are really encouraging clubs to look closely at their pricing, and make sure that they're actually meeting margins now, because clubs are facing the same costs as other licensed premises.

It's typically that they own their buildings. They're not paying monthly rents. But as I say, they still pay the breweries the same price as any other licensed premise. All right. Hey, thanks. If there's time, can I do one more? No. We're over time now.

Okay. Thank you. Hey, thanks, Lizzie. I really appreciate that. Thank you very much. Right. Now, we've got Alcohol Health Watch. I think we've got Andrew there. And Lizzie Barrett, is she coming in by Zoom, Andrew? We're both in person. Oh, you're here. Both of you. Good. Okay. Fantastic. We've got 10 minutes with you.

Tēnā koutou katoa. Ko Lizzie Barrett toku ingoa. I'm a health promotion advisor making this submission on behalf of Alcohol Health Watch. We're an independent NGO and peak body in Aotearoa with a focus on preventing and reducing alcohol-related harm by strengthening public policy. I'm joined by our executive director, Andrew Galloway.

Firstly, we give credit to our colleague, Sarah Sneed, who outlined the extensive evidence against most of this bill's proposed changes in our written submission. As you know, we oppose these changes because they prioritize reducing red tape for the alcohol industry over people's rights to health and wellbeing. Today, we'd like to emphasize our collective responsibility for a reasonable and evidence-based approach to alcohol law reform.

This should involve balancing the harms the alcohol industry causes against their ability to profit, keeping in mind that they do so through strategies that both normalize alcohol throughout our society and strategically profit off heavy drinking. As you've already heard this morning, the industry amplifies the harms of social disadvantage, including poverty, gendered violence, trauma, social isolation, and stigma and discrimination, just to name a few.

These dynamics mean that alcohol harm is a health, social, and justice issue with many different causes. But we can do something about the predatory strategies that disproportionately harm children, young people, and people with alcohol dependence. Unfortunately, this bill doesn't align with the solutions that most New Zealanders support.

Instead, we're concerned this bill will increase harm by increasing alcohol availability, diminishing people's right to have a say over alcohol-related issues in their area, and perversely increasing red tape for local councils and district licensing committees. The consequences of these changes, if enacted, would be felt by the friends, whanau, and parents losing their loved ones, including their children, to physical and mental health conditions.

The women and children experiencing alcohol-related violence. The people living with lifelong disabilities such as fetal alcohol spectrum disorder or impairments from alcohol-related accidents such as drunk driving. The bill's intentions don't just contradict the government-commissioned evidence on harm reduction that's been repeated since the 2010 Law Commission review.

They're a slap in the face to the people experiencing the consequences of a regulatory framework that drives lifelong, intergenerational, and inequitable harms that ultimately impact all of us. When alcohol is everywhere, these harms can and do happen to anyone, but it doesn't have to be this way. Most New Zealanders support strengthening our alcohol regulations, and we want to be very clear that this doesn't mean prohibition. Instead, it's about protecting our loved ones from harm.

And from the first day of submissions, we know even the alcohol industry agrees with changes that could do so. For example, 9:00 a.m. to 9:00 p.m. opening hours and stronger online delivery regulations, including a 24-hour safety pause between ordering and delivery. We also support the bill's proposal to strengthen online sales and delivery protections.

And while it's a modest start, we'd like to emphasize that this should extend to non-rapid delivery companies who mostly aren't checking IDs and are still doing same-day deliveries. Today, we're asking you to listen to the many people submitting on this bill who are brave enough to share their experiences with you, to value the knowledge and expertise that they've shared by taking it on board, and ultimately, to prevent the harms this bill could cause to people's lives by replacing it with the many other evidence-based, publicly supported

solutions that have been available to us for a long time, including having higher standards for the alcohol industry lobbying and alcohol sales and advertising, especially against strategies targeting children, young people, and highly deprived communities. Thank you for your time. We're happy to take questions.

Chair? Yes. Ms. Milich, Camilla. Thank you. Hopefully, you can hear me. Sorry to be online when you're there in person, but thank you so much for your submission. I just had a question around the impact that people who would be excluded from objecting under this bill currently have and the information that they may have available to raise objections under the act.

So obviously, under these changes, there would be a geographical limitation on people who could object, and I just wanted to know if you had any evidence or experience of people who sat outside these proposed geographic indicators who provided valuable information when licenses were being considered That's an excellent question, Member Bellich. Thank you.

We don't believe there should be any restriction on proximity of objectors. There wasn't under the 2023 amendment, and we've done analysis nationally and locally in Tamaki Makaurau, Auckland. The results are in our written submission, which show an incredibly low number of licenses being refused.

It is not a feature overseas in the jurisdictions we were able to find, and we haven't seen mischief that has, I think, been, you could say, mischievously alleged by the people who've brought this bill. We've seen people objecting for very good reason, parents of children in Dunedin who want to see less alcohol promotion, marketing, and advertising.

We've still got a parent of a child in Dunedin who perished, who died in a really tragic accident. You've got parents fronting up, Ben and Sarah Sims, whose child tragically died last year, very highly mediated and featured in Radio New Zealand and other media. There really shouldn't be any reason for limiting objections at all. That is our submission.

There hasn't been mischief from having it open to anyone. It is in line with other jurisdictions, including most states in Australia, the United Kingdom, and other like jurisdictions. And to do so is simply giving in to what the alcohol industry want. They don't want the annoyance of having to respond. But this is a system, if you remember at its principle, Sir Geoffrey Palmer and his team at the Law Commission did a very extensive and evidence-based review, the results of which came out in 2010, and one of the biggest principles of this bill was to give communities

more say. That was repeated by the then Justice Minister, Judith Collins, in a press release, saying this is about giving communities more say. You are turning that principle on its head with these changes. It's unnecessary. There hasn't been the mischief that's been alleged by the architects of this bill, and we reject the fact that there should be a limit on objections at all.
Right. Hey, thank you both to you, Andrew and Lizzie, for that presentation this morning. Very good to hear from you. Sure. No problem. Now, we're going to the Cancer Society, Rachel and Emma.

Good morning, both of you. We've got 10 minutes with you, as you know. Looking forward to hearing what you've got to say. Good morning. My name's Rachel Newman, and I'm joined here with Emma Shields, and she'll speak after me as well. We really appreciate the opportunity to come and present on behalf of the Cancer Society today.

We're deeply concerned that the bill will increase alcohol availability through a range of changes that might appear in isolation to be quite minor, but together will expand access and further normalize alcohol in our communities. When availability increases, we know that harm increases, and that includes cancer.

So alcohol is a well-established cause of at least seven different types of cancer, and this includes breast and bowel cancer. There is no safe limit of alcohol consumption in regards to cancer risk. And in New Zealand, cancer causes 1,200 cases each year, and these are entirely preventable cancer cases.

At the same time, we're facing a cancer crisis. Currently, around 30,000 people are diagnosed with cancer every year. But this has been projected to increase to more than 45,000 cancer cases every year. And when you look at our health system, it is already under significant strain in dealing with the current 30,000 cases.

We also know that alcohol harm and alcohol-related cancers are not evenly distributed in our communities. Maori and those living in more deprived communities experience a disproportionate burden of harm, worsening existing inequities in cancer outcomes. We also have strong evidence that increasing alcohol availability leads to increased consumption, which will lead to increased cancer rates.

So we're left with an inconsistency. On one hand, we have a government who's deeply committed to improving cancer outcomes by investing hundreds of millions of dollars in cancer medicines, lowering the bowel screening age, and opening new линей machines. And on the other hand, we have this bill that risks increasing our exposure to a known carcinogen in our communities and increasing the number of preventable cancers.

So from our perspective, we should all be aligned, including the government, on cancer outcomes and be taking every opportunity to reduce the number of new cancers and not implementing changes that will knowingly increase cancer rates in the future.

There is clear and consistent evidence that policies which reduce the availability, the affordability, and the promotion of alcohol are the most effective ways to reduce consumption and prevent alcohol-related cancers. On the other hand, measures that increase alcohol availability, such as expanding off licenses, enabling alcohol in new settings, or limiting community input into licensing decisions, will likely drive higher consumption over time and lead to increased alcohol-related cancer incidents.We are therefore very concerned with a number of the

proposed changes in this bill that may lead to greater alcohol availability and consumption, and ultimately contribute to these rising cancer rates in Aotearoa, which will impact families, communities across the country, and as Rachael said, place further pressure on our health system. So we strongly reiterate our position that alcohol regulation in Aotearoa should be strengthened, not weakened, to better protect the health of everyone.

Ensuring that communities retain a meaningful voice in alcohol decision-making, and limiting the widespread availability and normalization of alcohol are critical components of this. So thank you for your time today and for reading our written submission, and we would be happy to take questions from the committee.

Chair, I've got a question here. Good. Kel, Mr. Bay. Thanks, Chair. Thanks for the submission and thanks for the work that you do. I'm a big supporter and big volunteer up in Whanganui. Just on your argument, though, about alcohol consumption and widening the availability of making things like off licenses easier, et cetera. If you take that argument to its natural conclusion, it's prohibition, and we know that that doesn't work, right?

Earlier this morning, we've heard from Clubs New Zealand about, is the funeral going to be at this club or that venue or whatever. Then there's the emotional toll that it takes on licensees, and that's immeasurable. It would be fairer still, in my mind, particularly in Auckland, to tighten up the amendment to just those who can certify that they live or work within a kilometer of the premises to which the application relates.

One instance I had where an objector lived just at the cusp of two kilometers from a venue, and he was affected by the exterior color, so the visual amenity of the store when he traveled past it every now and again on a bus, and that was a valid objection, and that's kind of not fair.
